normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: No explicit preconditions are required by the implementation; it works for any input lists.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
let m := a.length; let n := b.length; ∃ (dp : Fin (m+1) → Fin (n+1) → Nat),
  (∀ i : Fin (m+1), dp i ⟨0, by simp⟩ = 0) ∧
  (∀ j : Fin (n+1), dp ⟨0, by simp⟩ j = 0) ∧
  (∀ i : Fin m, ∀ j : Fin n,
    dp ⟨i.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ i.2⟩ ⟨j.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ j.2⟩ =
      if a.get ⟨i.1, i.2⟩ = b.get ⟨j.1, j.2⟩ then
        dp ⟨i.1, i.2⟩ ⟨j.1, j.2⟩ + 1
      else
        Nat.max (dp ⟨i.1, i.2⟩ ⟨j.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ j.2⟩)
                 (dp ⟨i.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ i.2⟩ ⟨j.1, j.2⟩))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Model the DP table computed by the nested loops: dp[0][*]=0, dp[*][0]=0, and the recurrence for dp[i+1][j+1] matches the Rust code.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (a b : List Int),
  let m := a.length; let n := b.length;
  ∀ i : Fin m, ∀ j : Fin n,
    (a.get ⟨i.1, i.2⟩) = a.get ⟨i.1, i.2⟩ ∧ (b.get ⟨j.1, j.2⟩) = b.get ⟨j.1, j.2⟩
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The recurrence only accesses a[i-1] and b[j-1] for i∈1..m and j∈1..n; in Lean this corresponds to safe indexing via Fin.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (a b : List Int),
  let m := a.length; let n := b.length;
  ∀ (dp : Fin (m+1) → Fin (n+1) → Nat),
    (∀ i, dp i ⟨0, by simp⟩ = 0) →
    (∀ j, dp ⟨0, by simp⟩ j = 0) →
    (∀ i : Fin m, ∀ j : Fin n,
      dp ⟨i.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ i.2⟩ ⟨j.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ j.2⟩ =
        if a.get ⟨i.1, i.2⟩ = b.get ⟨j.1, j.2⟩ then
          dp ⟨i.1, i.2⟩ ⟨j.1, j.2⟩ + 1
        else
          Nat.max (dp ⟨i.1, i.2⟩ ⟨j.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ j.2⟩)
                   (dp ⟨i.1+1, by exact Nat.succ_lt_succ i.2⟩ ⟨j.1, j.2⟩)) →
    (∀ i : Fin (m+1), ∀ j : Fin (n+1),
      dp i j = lcsLen (a.take i.1) (b.take j.1))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove the DP table entry dp[i][j] equals the length of the longest common subsequence of the prefixes a[0..i) and b[0..j). (Here lcsLen is the standard LCS length function in Lean.)

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (a b : List Int),
  let m := a.length; let n := b.length;
  ∀ (dp : Fin (m+1) → Fin (n+1) → Nat),
    dp ⟨m, by simp⟩ ⟨n, by simp⟩ = lcsLen a b
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The function returns dp[m][n], so the returned value equals the LCS length of the full lists.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (a b : List Int) (i j : Nat),
  i ≤ a.length → j ≤ b.length →
  let ai := a.get ⟨i-1, by omega⟩; let bj := b.get ⟨j-1, by omega⟩;
  (ai = bj →
    lcsLen (a.take i) (b.take j) = lcsLen (a.take (i-1)) (b.take (j-1)) + 1) ∧
  (ai ≠ bj →
    lcsLen (a.take i) (b.take j) =
      Nat.max (lcsLen (a.take (i-1)) (b.take j)) (lcsLen (a.take i) (b.take (j-1))))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Justify the two cases in the recurrence: if the last elements of the prefixes match, LCS length increases by 1; otherwise it is the max of dropping one side.
The nested loops iterate over finite ranges 1..=m and 1..=n, so the computation is total.
